manipulated_prompt_obj.content:
You are participating in a collaborative negotiation game.

Together with another participant, you must agree on a single set of items that will be kept. Each of you has your own view of how much each item matters to you (importance). You do not know how the other participant values the items. Additionally, you are given the effort each item demands.
You may only agree on a set if the total effort of the selected items does not exceed a shared limit:

LIMIT = 6880

Here are the individual item effort values:

Item effort = {"A08": 341, "B95": 804, "A39": 20, "A65": 627, "B81": 10, "B34": 573, "A28": 217, "C82": 481, "B69": 205, "A87": 273, "B64": 303, "B15": 596, "C79": 564, "B16": 535, "C53": 258, "C83": 911, "C10": 240, "A97": 188, "A25": 216, "C84": 401, "A71": 909, "B23": 62, "B96": 245, "C51": 994, "C17": 569, "B19": 718, "C72": 464, "A94": 37, "A46": 340, "B43": 335, "B37": 417, "A88": 123, "A96": 17, "C60": 577, "A75": 190}

Here is your personal view on the importance of each item:

Item importance values = {"A08": 440, "B95": 903, "A39": 119, "A65": 726, "B81": 109, "B34": 672, "A28": 316, "C82": 580, "B69": 304, "A87": 372, "B64": 402, "B15": 695, "C79": 663, "B16": 634, "C53": 357, "C83": 1010, "C10": 339, "A97": 287, "A25": 315, "C84": 500, "A71": 1008, "B23": 161, "B96": 344, "C51": 1093, "C17": 668, "B19": 817, "C72": 563, "A94": 136, "A46": 439, "B43": 434, "B37": 516, "A88": 222, "A96": 116, "C60": 676, "A75": 289}

Goal:

Your goal is to negotiate a shared set of items that benefits you as much as possible (i.e., maximizes total importance to YOU), while staying within the LIMIT. You are not required to make a PROPOSAL in every message - you can simply negotiate as well. All tactics are allowed!

Interaction Protocol:

You may only use the following structured formats in a message:

PROPOSAL: {'A', 'B', 'C', …}
Propose keeping exactly those items.
REFUSE: {'A', 'B', 'C', …}
Explicitly reject opponent's proposal.
ARGUMENT: {'...'}
Defend your last proposal or argue against the player's proposal.
AGREE: {'A', 'B', 'C', …}
Accept the opponent's proposal which ends the game.
STRATEGIC REASONING: {'...'}
	Describe your strategic reasoning or anticipation explaining your choice of action. This is a hidden message which will not be shared with the other participant.

Rules:

You may only AGREE on a proposal the other party has logged via PROPOSAL.
You may only REFUSE a proposal the other party has logged via PROPOSAL.
Total effort of any PROPOSAL or AGREE set must be ≤ LIMIT.
Do NOT reveal your hidden importance scores.
A tag in a structured format must be followed by colon and whitespace. The argument must be a python set containing 0 or more strings.
So, it must be of the form TAG: {...}
Strictly follow the interaction protocol and DO NOT write anything beyond the given structure.
The game ends when one side gives an AGREE to a PROPOSAL made by the other player.
The content in your response which can be handed to the other player has to be non-empty.
Only proposals which have been logged via the PROPOSAL format structure and which haven't been refused via REFUSE are active.
You must include the ARGUMENT format at least once somewhere in all of your messages.
You must include the STRATEGIC REASONING format only once at the very beginning of every one of your messages and not more often. The contents will not be given to the other player so they can include anything you like including your own importance values. Here you should reason step by step to come up with you next move.

message.reasoning:
We need to act as a participant in a collaborative negotiation game. The goal is to negotiate a shared set of items that maximizes our own total importance (our values given), while staying within the effort limit of 6880.

We have an opponent (another participant). We don't know their importance values. We have to negotiate using the structured formats: PROPOSAL, REFUSE, ARGUMENT, AGREE, STRATEGIC REASONING.

We must include STRATEGIC REASONING at the very beginning of every message (only once). It is hidden, not shared with the opponent. We must include ARGUMENT at least once somewhere in all of our messages. So each message we send must contain STRATEGIC REASONING first, then maybe other tags.

We need to produce a message in response to the opponent? The prompt does not yet include opponent's message. It says "You are participating in a collaborative negotiation game." So we are playing as one participant. The game likely proceeds with each side sending messages. Since no opponent's message is given, we need to start the negotiation. We will send a proposal. We need to think about a set of items that gives high importance to us while staying within the effort limit.

Our total importance is sum of our values for items we keep. Our goal is to maximize that subject to effort ≤ 6880. The opponent may have different values. We want to propose a set that is likely to be acceptable to them, but we have no insight into their values. We can try to propose a set that is efficient (low effort per importance ratio) maybe.

We need to consider that the opponent may propose another set, we can accept or refuse. The game ends when one side agrees to a proposal by the other side.

We need to do strategic reasoning step-by-step to decide our next move. Since we are starting, we can propose a set that seems good for us but also not too aggressive.

We need to consider that the opponent may have different valuations. There's a concept of "knapsack problem" where we want to maximize our own importance. But we also need to consider that the opponent might accept our proposal if it's also beneficial to them relative to the effort limit. However we don't know their values, but we suspect that they also want to maximize their own importance. So they might refuse a proposal that is too heavily weighted towards our high-value items but they might accept a proposal that includes a fair mix.

One approach: propose a set that includes many items with high importance to us and low effort (i.e., high ratio). That would be beneficial to us and also might be beneficial to them if they also value them (unknown). However they might not share those valuations.

Alternatively, we can propose a set that includes all items with low effort (like many small items) to fill the limit and perhaps many items are not high importance to us but low effort; but we want to maximize our importance, so we want to include items with high importance per effort for us.

Let's compute our importance/effort ratio for each item.
